coredata.dc:title: Highly selective and sensitive detection of calcium (II) ions in human serum using novel fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ankireddy, Seshadri Reddy
Kim, Jongsung
coredata.dc:description: A unique and facile biosensing system for the detection of low-concentration divalent calcium ions (Ca2+) in human serum was developed using an environmentally benign, blue-colored fluorescent multi-carboxylic acid-functionalized carbon dots (CDs) that were synthesized from ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) via a hydrothermal process. These CDs are termed as ECDs. Our results showed that this newly developed strategy can offer an expeditious, highly sensitive, and selective detection of Ca2+ ions in human serum, with a detection limit as low as 77pM and a potent working range of 1–10nM. The Ca2+ ions interact with the carboxylic groups of the ECDs, could be resulting in a strong photoluminescence quenching of the ECDs via a non-radiative electron or energy transfer process. Furthermore, the detection of Ca2+ ions in human serum sample obtained using this sensing system was in good agreement with results derived from inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry analysis. No significant PL quenching was observed with other ionic species such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ owing to their monovalent nature and the fact that they typically exist in human serum at very low concentrations, which disfavors the formation of their corresponding complexes on the surface of the ECDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Human serum
EDTA
Photoluminescence quenching
Electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Human serum pretreatment and sample preparation
FTIR and Raman analysis of ECDs
Sensing of Ca2+ ions in human serum using ECDs
Plausible PL quenching mechanism of the ECDs with Ca2+ ions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF2017R1D1A1B04035070 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of ECDs from EDTA using a hydrothermal treatment method for the selective and sensitive detection of Ca2+ ions via PL quenching.

(A) Low-resolution TEM, (B) HRTEM images of the ECDs, (C) Gaussian statistical size analysis of the nanoparticles, (d) XRD pattern of the ECDs. The inset in (B) shows the SAED pattern of crystallinity of the ECDs.
XPS spectra of the ECDs. (A) Total survey, high-resolution XPS spectra of the ECDs, (B) C1s, (C) N1s, (D) O1s, and (E) Ca 2p.

A (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the ECDs on their own, and (b, c) in the presence of Ca2+ ions in DI water and human serum, respectively. (d, e) PL maximum excitation and emission spectra of the ECDs (λex =353nm, λem =450nm). (f) PL spectra of the ECDs in the presence of Ca2+ ions. The inset shows an image of the ECDs: (i) under bright field, (ii) in the absence, and (iii) in the presence of Ca2+ ions under UV illumination at 350nm (the concentration of Ca2+ ions is 10nM), and (B) Excitation-dependent PL emission spectra of ECDs, obtained at increasing excitation wavelengths (from 300 to 560nm).

(A, B) PL emission spectra of ECDs (0.12mg mL−1) in 500μL of PBS buffer with different concentrations of Ca2+ ions in DI water and human serum at 0, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10nM. The figure inset shows the linear relationship curves between I0/I and the concentrations of Ca2+ ions in DI water and human serum.

Selectivity and inhibitor efficiency as a function of various potential interferences over the Ca2+ ions in DI water and human serum. The concentrations of the ECDs and various metal ions are 0.12mgmL−1 ECDs in 500μL of PBS buffer and 10nM, respectively. The figure inset shows the corresponding solutions under UV illumination.
Schematic representation of a plausible sensing mechanism of the ECDs for Ca2+ ions.
Comparison of various methods for the detection of Ca2+ ions in human serum.

head.ce:title: Highly selective and sensitive detection of calcium (II) ions in human serum using novel fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, Seongnam, Gyeonggi-Do 461-701, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A unique and facile biosensing system for the detection of low-concentration divalent calcium ions (Ca2+) in human serum was developed using an environmentally benign, blue-colored fluorescent multi-carboxylic acid-functionalized carbon dots (CDs) that were synthesized from ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) via a hydrothermal process. These CDs are termed as ECDs. Our results showed that this newly developed strategy can offer an expeditious, highly sensitive, and selective detection of Ca2+ ions in human serum, with a detection limit as low as 77pM and a potent working range of 1–10nM. The Ca2+ ions interact with the carboxylic groups of the ECDs, could be resulting in a strong photoluminescence quenching of the ECDs via a non-radiative electron or energy transfer process. Furthermore, the detection of Ca2+ ions in human serum sample obtained using this sensing system was in good agreement with results derived from inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry analysis. No significant PL quenching was observed with other ionic species such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ owing to their monovalent nature and the fact that they typically exist in human serum at very low concentrations, which disfavors the formation of their corresponding complexes on the surface of the ECDs.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of biomolecular cellular functions with metal ions using fluorescent materials such as quantum dots (QDs), is becoming much easier owing to their intensive development for biosensing and medical applications [1–4]. The CdSe/ZnS QDs are a potential candidate for the condensation and decondensation of plasmid DNAs [5], as light-controlled sensors for the detection of hydrogen peroxide [6], and for the molecular imaging of living cells [7,8]. There have been several reports on the use of quantum dots like CdSe, CdSe/ZnS, and CdTe even though they have been shown to be hazardous to human health and environment [9,10]. But from the last few years, non-toxic fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as an outstanding candidates to replace toxic fluorescent materials for the biosensing and bioimaging purposes. This is due to their excellent water solubility, high quantum yield, resistance to photo leeching, robust biocompatibility, and good photoluminescence (PL) properties [11–18]. CQDs are a unique class of fluorescent materials, with powerful photocatalytic and photophysical properties [19–22]. Numerous methods for the preparation of CQDs have been reported and they include electrochemical [20], hydrothermal, and pyrolysis syntheses [23–25]. Among these, the hydrothermal method is well-established owing to the perfect reorganization and reorientation of functional groups on the surface of CQDs during the process of hydrothermal reaction.

Calcium is one of the most abundant divalent inorganic elements in the human body, and involves in numerous biochemical reactions such as bone formation, hormone-mediated stimulation, blood pressure control, blood coagulation, neurotransmission signaling, cell signaling, as well as the excitation of skeletal and cardiac muscle at molecular levels [26–29]. In addition, several diseases are also associated with the molecular concentrations of calcium ions. Oxidative stress and apoptosis are conditions associated with elevated levels of intracellular calcium ions, while long-term calcium deficiency also leads to rickets and poor blood clotting function. Moreover, osteoporosis occurs as a result of very low intracellular calcium levels (particularly in women), leading to bone deterioration and an increased risk of bone fractures [30–34].

Over the past three decades, several analytical techniques have been developed for the quantification and detection of calcium ions in human serum, including atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [35], colorimetric method [36], ion-selective electrode method [37], and capillary electrophoresis with contactless conductivity detection [38]. However, the aforementioned analytical methods may not be completely suitable for the detection of calcium in human serum as they require sophisticated equipment and involve time-consuming and tedious handling, as well as difficult sample preparation processes. In contrast, using a fluorescence assay to detect Ca2+ ions in human serum has several advantages such as high selectivity and sensitivity, along with fast analysis time, when compared to other methods. Currently, several commercial kits are available for the detection of Ca2+ ions by using fluorimetric method with few disadvantages such as less stable at room temperature, light sensitive, and quite expensive to purchase compared with present sensing system [39–41]. Therefore, in order to effectively monitor the onset or progression of serious human diseases, it is necessary to develop an economical, simple, stable, fast, and reliable protocol using non-toxic fluorescent materials such as CQDs. In this study, we report the synthesis of environmentally benign, hydrophilic, and highly fluorescent ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA)-CDs (ECDs) through a single-step hydrothermal process using EDTA (a commonly available laboratory chemical) as a carbon source with carboxylic acid functional groups. The synthesized ECDs not only have an excellent fluorescence activity but more importantly, are suited to chemical sensing owing to the carboxylic acid functional groups on their surface [42]. The absorption and emission peaks of the newly synthesized ECDs appear at 353 and 450nm, respectively, and have a high quantum yield of ∼22%. To the best of our knowledge, there are currently no published reports on a fluorescence quenching process that has a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 77pM. Our biosensor has been developed for the ultra-sensitive and selective detection of Ca2+ ions in human serum involving a complexation reaction between carboxylic acid and Ca2+ ions, which suggests that ECDs can be used for the detection of different diseases.

Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), iron(II) chloride (FeCl2), iron(III) chloride (FeCl3), and human serum (human male AB plasma) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. For pH measurements, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solutions with different pH values were prepared using 0.05molL−1 of phosphoric acid and 0.01molL−1 of sodium hydroxide solutions (i.e., mixtures adjusted to the respective required pH values). Deionized (DI) water was used for all the experiments. All chemicals were used directly without any further purification.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) spectra were recorded using Varian Cary 100 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Meanwhile, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a high-resolution Bruker Vertex 70 FTIR spectrometer. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of the as-prepared ECDs was performed using a Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200kV electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were taken by a Sigma Probe spectrometer (Thermo VG), equipped with an A1-Kα source (15kV, 100W, 400μm). X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies (Scintag-SDS 2000) were performed at 40kV and 30mA in the continuous-scanning 2θ mode. Different pH solutions were prepared using a Thermo Scientific pH meter. All fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 353nm at room temperature using a fluoroluminescence spectrometer (Quanta Master, Photon Technology International, NJ, USA), equipped with a Xe lamp (Arc Lamp Housing, A-1010B™), monochromator, and power supply (Brytexbox). The surface charge distribution of the as-synthesized ECDs was determined using an ELSZ-2000 series zeta-potential and particle size analyzer. The concentration of Ca2+ ions was determined via inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) using a Perkin-Elmer SCIEX NexION 350D mass spectrometer, equipped with an argon plasma operating at 6000K. The quantum yield (QY) was recorded using a Fluoro-Q2100 quantum yield system (at 353nm excitation wavelength) and calculated according to the following equation.
(1)
Q u a n t u m
Y i e l d =
E
c
L a
−
L c
where, Ec is the emission produced by direct excitation light, La is the total amount of excitation light, and Lc is the amount of light measured after direct excitation [43]. The instrument response function (IRF) was recorded using a 1% LUDOX® LS colloidal silica suspension in water as the background signal. All fluorescence lifetime decay curves were recorded using the EasyLife II fluorometer system (utilizing the EL series of nanosecond pulsed light-emitting diodes (LEDs) by Photon Technology International), with auto adjustable excitation wavelengths ranging from 260 to 650nm. The curve fitting data were obtained using the EasyLife II program via a serial RS232 and USB connection.

Multi-carboxylic acid-functionalized ECDs were easily synthesized by a hydrothermal process using EDTA, a commonly available laboratory chemical. In a typical synthetic procedure, 200mg of EDTA was mixed with 25mL of DI water, and sonicated the mixture for 15min. The reaction mixture was then transferred into a 50-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 170°C for 180min. After heating, the Teflon-lined autoclave was placed in a fume hood and allowed to cool down at room temperature, before transferring the resulting yellow solution into a 100-mL round-bottomed flask. The pH of the reaction mixture was maintained at 8 by adding ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) and the mixture was refluxed for 3h. The color of the reaction mixture changed from light yellow to light brown, which indicated the formation of the desired ECDs. The formed ECDs were separated by centrifugation (8000rpm, 15min) and dialyzed with a cellulose ester membrane (molecular weight cut-off [MWCO]: 1.5kDa) against DI water for 12h. The precipitated ECDs were re-dispersed into DI water and kept in the refrigerator for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Human serum pretreatment and sample preparation
ce:section.ce:para: Healthy human serum sample from Sigma-Aldrich was selected as the test sample as it contains an adequate amount of Ca2+ ions that can be used for analysis at the molecular level. Approximately 5mL of human serum was mixed with 9mL of 0.1% of nitric acid and sonicated for 15min. The reaction mixture was then heated on a hot plate at 180°C for 4h and the sample’s pH was adjusted to 7.2. Next, the solution was dialyzed with cellulose ester dialysis membrane (MWCO: 500–1000Da) for 24h to release the bound Ca2+ ions into the serum.

The detection of Ca2+ ions was achieved using fluorometric assay, and all spectra were recorded at room temperature. In a typical run, 100μL of Ca2+ ion solution (at different concentrations) and 500μL of PBS buffer solution were added into a cuvette containing 0.12mgL−1 of ECDs. The same procedure was also applied for the detection of Ca2+ ions from the human serum. From the above solution, very less dilution of three unknown concentrations were prepared. The amount of Ca2+ ions in the unknown samples were calculated by calibration curve based on ECDs standard solution in PBS. The reaction mixture was incubated for 15–30min in dark after addition of Ca2+ into the system and all PL spectra were recorded under excitation at 353nm.

In this paper, we have developed a facile one pot synthesis of polar multi-carboxylic acid-functionalized ECDs from EDTA and the reaction scheme is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Briefly, ECDs were synthesized from EDTA by undergoing a hydrothermal treatment at 170°C in a 50-mL Teflon-lined autoclave for 180min. The color of the solution turned brown when the reaction mixture was refluxed with ammonium hydroxide at pH 8. Strongly blue-colored fluorescent-emitting ECDs with multi-carboxylic acid functionalities were formed passively after 3h of reflux. Finally, the ECDs were separated by ultra-centrifugation with DI water (i.e., the process was repeated thrice) and the obtained ECDs were stored in the refrigerator for future use.

HRTEM was used for the morphological examination of the ECDs as depicted in Fig. 2 . According to the TEM results, all ECDs were uniformly dispersed, with an average size of 3–5nm as shown in Fig. 2A. Fig. 2B, shows the well-resolved poor crystalline HRTEM image of a carbon dots with perfect discernibility of lattice spacing, along with the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) of the ECD nanoparticles. The average distance between two lattice planes were found to be 0.2248nm, which corresponded to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic (sp2) carbon [44]. Meanwhile, Fig. 2C, shows the TEM-based statistical size distribution of the ECDs, which is further illustrated by a particle size histogram that shows a mean diameter of 2–6nm. XRD analysis was used to verify the formation of the ECDs based on the position of the respective diffraction peaks, as shown in Fig. 2D. The broad and centered XRD diffraction peak was observed in the range of 19.14°, which corresponded to a distorted graphitic structure. The data was also in good agreement with the measured distance between the two lattice planes observed via HRTEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR and Raman analysis of ECDs
ce:section.ce:para: The respective functional groups and their changes after the addition of different ions were distinguished using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. The FTIR spectra of EDTA, ECDs, and the interaction of Ca2+ ions with the ECDs are depicted in (Fig. S1) of the Supplementary Information. With the exception of curve (a), additional absorption peaks were observed at 3090, 1530 and 840cm−1 in all other curves, which were ascribed to the aromatic CH stretching, CC, and CH out-of-plane bending modes, respectively. This clearly confirms the derivatization of ECDs with a graphitic structure from EDTA, and the additional peak observed at 3350cm−1 was attributed to the amine (NH) stretching frequency. This peak appeared owing to presence of the amine functionality and it is in good agreement with the XPS (NH) results. In curve (a), the characteristic absorption peaks of carbonyl (CO) and CN stretching frequencies were observed at 1740 and 1240cm−1, respectively, with the CN stretching frequency being observed in all of the recorded curves. The coordination of metal ions with the carboxylates was determined by the Δv of the carboxylato co-ordination. In the spectrum of curve (b), the v asym (COO) and v sym (COO) modes were observed at 1650 and 1710cm−1, respectively (Δv =60cm−1). After incorporating Ca2+ ions into the ECDs (curve [c]), the spectrum of the compound showed a distinct shift of the v asym (COO) band to a higher frequency (from 1650 to 1840cm−1) while the v sym (COO) band was shifted a lower region (1630cm−1). The resulting Δv (210cm−1 for Ca2+ ions) was much greater in value relative to that of free ECDs, thus clearly indicating the presence of unidentate coordinated carboxylate groups in the compound. Ca2+ ions are involved in a hexa-coordination with four oxygen atoms of carboxylates and two nitrogen atom lone pairs are also involved in the coordination process to form an octahedral complex as depicted in Fig. 1 [45,46]. These carboxylate peaks also coincide with the CO, OCO, and COH peaks of O1s from the XPS results. Owing to presence of these polar functional groups, the as-synthesized ECDs are highly soluble in double-distilled water. Based on the Raman spectra of the ECDs, the characteristic D and G bands observed at 1385 and 1535cm−1, respectively, are attributed to the sp2 hybridized CC bond as depicted in (Fig. S2) of the Supplementary Information.

Functional group, elemental, and surface composition analyses of the as-synthesized ECDs were achieved using the XPS analytical technique. The XPS spectrum of the ECDs revealed three peaks at 285.5, 399.4 and 531.9eV, which were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. This finding indicates that the ECDs mainly consist of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen (Fig. 3 A, line i). In addition to the respective peaks for carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, a new peak that corresponds to Ca 2p also appeared at 346eV (Fig. 3A, line ii). The decreasing peak intensities of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen after the addition of Ca2+ ions suggests that the Ca2+ ions are well coordinated to the carboxylate functional groups on the surface of the ECDs. The deconvolutions of the high-resolution XPS data of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Ca 2p are shown in Fig. 3B–E. The deconvolution of the C 1s spectrum of the bare ECDs (Fig. 3B) revealed the presence of three peaks that correspond to the CC (sp2), CN/CO, and CO groups at 284.3, 285.9, and 287.8eV, respectively. Meanwhile, the high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) showed two peaks at 398.6 and 400.8eV, which were assigned to the CNC and NH groups, respectively, whereas the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3D) revealed three peaks at 530.5, 531.8, and 532.9eV owing to the presence of the CO, OCO, and COH groups, respectively [47]. In addition, a doublet peak at 346.4eV (Ca 2p3/2) and 350.05eV (Ca 2p1/2) was evident in the high-resolution Ca 2p spectrum (Fig. 3E), indicating the presence of Ca ions after treating the ECDs with Ca2+ ions. Based on the results of this investigation, we can confirm that the Ca2+ ions are well coordinated to the carboxylate functional groups on the surface of the ECDs.

The optical properties of the ECDs in aqueous solution were investigated and illustrated in Fig. 4 . Owing to the presence of active polar functional groups on the ECDs, a strong absorption peak was observed between 310 and 325nm as shown in curves (a), (b), and (c) of Fig. 4A. Interestingly, an additional peak was also observed in the range of 395–400nm for the ECDs after the addition of 10nM of Ca2+ ions in both DI water and human serum, as shown in curves (b) and (c). The same peak was not observed in curve (a) due to the coordination of the divalent Ca2+ ions to the multi-carboxylic acid functional groups on the surface of the ECDs to form an octahedral complex. This observation clearly indicates the presence of carboxylic acids on the surface of the ECDs and these absorption peaks appeared owing to the n-π* and π-π* transitions of the carboxylic acid and amine functional groups. Curves (d) and (e) show a strongly blue-colored fluorescence of the as-synthesized ECDs with maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 353 and 450nm, respectively. The PL intensity of the ECDs was approximately 99.91% quenched after the addition of 10nM of Ca2+ ions as shown in curve (f). The inset of Fig. 4A shows the bright field (i) and dark field images of the ECDs before (ii) and after (iii) adding 10nM of Ca2+ ions. The as-synthesized ECDs have dynamic excitation-dependent PL properties in the visible range, which are useful for bio-imaging applications as shown in Fig. 4B. When the excitation wavelength was varied between 300 and 560nm, the PL emission was red-shifted from 425 to 580nm. Moreover, the emission peak intensity gradually increased and reached a maximum at 450nm under an excitation wavelength of 353nm, before decreasing slightly thereafter. The excitation-dependent PL behavior may be attributed to the presence of some crystal defects and differently sized nanoparticles, which have different band gaps [48].

The PL stability of the ECDs at various NaCl concentrations was investigated in a PBS buffer as depicted in (Fig. S3) of the Supplementary Information. A significant change in PL intensity was not observed by varying the NaCl concentrations. This suggests that these ECDs are stable at different physiological conditions and are well-suited for sensing and bio-imaging applications. The pH dependency of the PL intensity is illustrated in (Fig. S4) of the Supplementary Information. The as-synthesized ECDs are stable from pH 4.0–8.5, and this pH range is suitable for bio-imaging applications. In extreme acidic conditions, the PL intensity of the ECDs was quenched by 35% with the addition of 10nM Ca2+ ions from human serum and by 62% in the absence of these Ca2+ ions. All carboxylate groups on the surface of the ECDs are protonated in a highly acidic medium. Owing to the protonated carboxylate groups, the formation of a complex between the surface carboxylates and the Ca2+ ions on the surface of the ECDs was interrupted. Meanwhile, in extreme alkaline medium, the PL intensity of the ECDs was quenched by 54% in DI water and by 99.2% in 10nM of Ca2+ ions from human serum as all carboxylate groups on the surface of the ECDs remain as anions, which is ideal for their coordination to Ca2+ ions and the subsequent formation of an octahedral complex. The PL intensity quenching occurs via a photo-induced electron transfer (PIET) from the conductance band (CB) of the ECDs to the newly formed complex. In contrast, in weakly acidic media (pH 4−7.2), recorded quenching efficiencies have similarly high values, suggesting that this pH range can be used for the sensitive detection of Ca2+ ions in human serum. (Fig. S5) of the Supplementary Information shows the stability of the ECDs’ PL intensity in a PBS buffer; the PL intensity was maintained within 98% for up to 120days. The inset figure shows an image of ECDs (0.12mgmL−1) in PBS buffer under UV illumination at different time intervals, from 0 to 120days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Ca2+ ions in human serum using ECDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The detection of Ca2+ ions in DI water and human serum using the fluorescence analysis of ECDs is illustrated in Fig. 5 A and B, respectively. The figure shows that the PL intensity of the ECDs gradually decreases upon the addition of Ca2+ ions (at different concentrations) in DI water and human serum, ranging from 1 to 10nM. The PL intensity was quenched by 99.92% and 99.94% after adding 10nM of Ca2+ ions in DI water and in human serum, respectively. The figure inset shows the corresponding response of normalized fluorescence intensity of the ECDs (I0 /I) versus the concentration of Ca2+ ions (I0 and I represent the PL intensities in the absence and presence of Ca2+ ions, respectively). The Stern-Volmer equation was applied to investigate the relationship between the quenching efficiency of the ECDs and the concentration of the Ca2+ ions [49].
(2)
I
0
I
= 1 + K q
[ Q ]
where, I0 and I represent fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Ca2+ ions, respectively. Q is the concentration of the Ca2+ ions and Kq is the quenching constant of the Ca2+ ions. The insets of Fig. 5A and B show that a linear relationship between I0 /I and the concentration of Ca2+ ions could be observed when the latter ranges from 1 to 10nM, with a coefficient of determination (r2 ) for line fitting that is close to 1, i.e. r2
=0.9918 and 0.9965 in the presence of Ca2+ ions in DI water and human serum, respectively. The ECDs showed a highly linear relationship with Ca2+ ions in human serum in comparison to Ca2+ ions in DI water, which clearly suggested that our sensing system has a higher sensitivity in detecting Ca2+ ions in human serum. The limit of detection (calculated according to a signal-to-noise ratio of S/N=3) for Ca2+ ions in DI water and human serum was found to be as low as 92 and 77 pM, respectively. According to these results, our sensing system is suitable for the determination of Ca2+ ions in DI water and human serum within a wide concentration range of 0.077–10nM based on the detection of fluorescence intensity. Using ICP-MS analysis, the concentrations of Ca2+, Mg2+, and Fe3+ ions in a human serum sample were found to be 2.01±0.038, 0.06±0.054, and 0.00209±0.062mM, respectively. Based on the ECDs standard curve, the unknown samples concentrations were found to be 3.8±0.09, 7.13±0.11, and 9.2±0.18nM. In addition, according to the ICP-MS analysis, the unknown samples concentrations were also found 3.77±0.19, 7.26±0.31, 9.4±0.08nM. These results were well agreement with the ICP-MS analysis. The obtained results confirmed the validity of this ECD-based PL sensing method for the selective and sensitive detection of Ca2+ ions in human serum. The experimental results were compared to other published reports on the detection of Ca2+ ions in human serum (Table 1 ) and the current method is a good alternative to the existing methods with a limit of detection as low as 77 pM.

In order to explore the selectivity of Ca2+ ion detection in human serum, interferences that could affect the PL of the ECDs in human serum were investigated. Fig. 6 shows a series of selectivity experiments involving various ions (at different concentrations) that are present in human serum. The figure inset shows the complete PL quenching of the ECDs in the presence of Ca2+ ions in both DI water and human serum while no significant fluorescence quenching was observed for Na+, K+, Mg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ ions at pH 7.2, thus clearly demonstrating the selectivity of this sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible PL quenching mechanism of the ECDs with Ca2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Monitoring the surface charge of the as-synthesized ECDs before and after the addition of Ca2+ ions using zeta potential (ζ) analysis will elucidate the complex formation process on the surface of the ECDs. The zeta potential (ζ) of the as-synthesized ECDs were found to be −10.09mV, which clearly indicates that the surface of the ECDs is fully functionalized with negatively charged functional groups such as carboxylic acids. After the addition of Ca2+ ions, the zeta potential (ζ) dramatically decreased to −2.42mV due to the formation of a [Ca-EDTA]2− octahedral complex on the surface of the ECDs. The UV–vis spectrum also revealed peaks at 310–320nm and 395–400nm, which corresponded to before and after the addition of Ca2+ ions to the as-synthesized ECDs, respectively. Based on the aforementioned results, we proposed that a plausible working mechanism for the PL quenching of the ECDs, in the presence of Ca2+ ions, could occurs via a non-radiative electron or energy transfer process as depicted in Fig. 7 . The band gap (Eg ) of the as-synthesized ECDs and that of the ECD complex were calculated using the diffusion reflectance (DR) spectra of the samples after undergoing the Kubelka-Munk treatment, as shown in (Fig. S6 A and B) [51]. The Eg of the as-synthesized ECDs and that of the ECD-Ca2+ complex was determined to be 1.65 and 0.95eV, respectively. In the absence of Ca2+ ions, the PL intensity was high but it was readily quenched after the addition of Ca2+ ions owing to the formation of a [Ca-EDTA]2− hexa-coordination complex on the surface of the ECDs [42]. As a result of the excited state of the complex, the band gap is reduced when compared to neat ECDs. Therefore, electrons of the ECD complex, which exist in the excited state, are partially transferred from the CB to the empty 3d orbital of Ca2+ ions, leading to fluorescence quenching via a non-radiative electron or energy transfer process.

Finally, the PL quenching mechanism of the ECD complex to the [Ca-EDTA]2− octahedral complex was confirmed using time-resolved PL lifetime decay studies. (Fig. S7) of the Supplementary Information shows the time-resolved PL lifetime decay analysis of ECDs in the presence as well as the absence of 10nM Ca2+ ions originating from human serum (the spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 353nm). The fluorescence decay of all the curves can be fitted using a double exponential function according to following expression:
(3)
D
( t )
=
Σ
i = 1
n
a i
exp
( −
t
τ i
)
where, D is PL decay, τ is life time, τi is the PL lifetimes of various fluorescent forms, and ai is the corresponding pre-exponential factor. The exact mechanism of lifetime decay is presently unclear. However, the lifetime of ECDs has been experimentally shown to exist in the range of 1–5ns, which is in good agreement with the lifetime of CQDs and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in published literature [52]. Interestingly, the lifetime of ECDs decreased from 0.189 (τ1 ) and 5.469ns (τ2 ) to 0.062 (τ1 ) and 1.07ns (τ2 ) after the addition of human serum Ca2+ ions, which clearly indicates that the electron transfers from the CB of the ECDs to the [Ca-EDTA]2− octahedral complex on its surface, may resulted in the PL quenching of the ECDs via a non-radiative electron or energy transfer process. These results support the use of the Ca2+ probe as an effective analytical tool for detecting Ca2+ ions in human serum.

In summary, we have synthesized ECDs whose surface contains multi-carboxylate functional groups via a simple hydrothermal reaction using EDTA as a starting material. The synthesized ECDs exhibited excellent blue color PL emission, along with high stability and high quantum yield (∼22%). The as-synthesized ECDs serve as a unique, novel, and fluorescent probe for the selective and sensitive detection of Ca2+ ions in human serum. Ca2+ ions react with multi-carboxylate functional groups on the surface of the ECDs to form an octahedral complex, which then leads to PL quenching via a photo-induced electron transfer process. Based on our study, an excellent ECD-based PL sensing probe was successfully developed for the detection of Ca2+ ions in human serum with a limit of detection as low as 77 pM and a potent working range of 1–10nM. This kind of environmentally benign PL-based sensing system has several distinct advantages, which include relatively easy and cheap preparation procedures, rapid analyte detection, as well as high selectivity and sensitivity. The cheap but precise detection of Ca2+ ions at very low concentrations will undoubtedly improve the diagnosis of several human diseases such as rickets, osteoporosis, and a few other neurological disorders.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF2017R1D1A1B04035070).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.09.172.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Immobilization of quantum dots via conjugated self-assembled monolayers and their application as a light-controlled sensor for the detection of hydrogen peroxide
Quantum dot targeting with lipoic acid ligase and HaloTag for single-molecule imaging on living cells
Selecting improved peptidyl motifs for cytosolic delivery of disparate protein and nanoparticle materials
A pilot study in non-human primates shows no adverse response to intravenous injection of quantum dots
Understanding biophysicochemical interactions at the nano–bio interface
Carbon quantum dots for fluorescence labeling of cells
Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers

Synthesis of glycine-functionalized graphene quantum dots as highly sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor of ascorbic acid in human serum

Pee-dots: biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots derived from the upcycling of urine

Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Carbon quantum dots/Cu2O composites with protruding nanostructures and their highly efficient (near) infrared photocatalytic behavior

Formation mechanism of carbogenic nanoparticles with dual photoluminescence emission
Simple aqueous solution route to luminescent carbogenic dots from carbohydrates
Nano-carrier for gene delivery and bioimaging based on carbon dots with PEI-passivation enhanced fluorescence
Calcium pumps in health and disease
Olfactory control of blood progenitor maintenance
Sources and deficiency diseases of mineral nutrients in human health and nutrition: a review
Melatonin counteracts alterations in oxidative metabolism and cell viability induced by intracellular calcium overload in human leucocytes: changes with age
Relation of alleles of the collagen type Iα1 gene to bone density and the risk of osteoporotic fractures in postmenopausal women
Measurement of total calcium in serum by atomic absorption spectrophotometry, with use of a strontium internal reference
Bioinspired colorimetric detection of calcium (II) ions in serum using calsequestrin-functionalized gold nanoparticles
Neutral carrier based ion-selective electrode for the determination of total calcium in blood serum

Engineering surface states of carbon dots to achieve controllable luminescence for solid-luminescent composites and sensitive Be2+ detection

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Ankireddy Seshadri Reddy completed his Ph.D under the supervision of Prof. Jongsung Kim in 2017 and currently serving as Assistant Professor in the Department of Chemical & Biological Engineering, Gachon University, Seoul, South Korea. His research interests include nanomaterials and MOF- mediated biosensing, drug delivery, catalysis and renewable energy.

Prof. Jongsung Kim received his Ph.D in 1997 from Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, USA. He is currently serving as a Professor in the Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, South Korea. His research interests include nano materials, bio sensors, and renewable energy.
